title: The Saturday Shop
summary: Leo helps his father in a small shop in a small town.
coverScene: a cozy little bookshop with a child reading aloud to other children sitting in a circle by the window

sentences.text: Leo is sixteen years old.
He lives in a small town.
His father has a little shop.
The shop sells books and paper.
Leo helps there after school.
He knows every book in the shop.
A big new store opens in town.
It is very big and full of light.
Now people go there every day.
Not many people come to the little shop.
Some days no one comes at all.
Leo's father sits and looks at the door.
He does not say much now.
Leo sees that his father is not happy.
He wants to help him.
But what can a child do?
He thinks about it every day.
One day, Leo watches the people outside.
Mothers and fathers go to the big store.
Their children wait outside it.
The children have nothing to do.
Then Leo has an idea.
On Saturday morning, Leo opens the shop early.
He puts small chairs near the window.
He puts them in a little circle.
He takes a book with good stories.
Then he starts to read out loud.
One child comes in and sits down.
Then three more children come.
Leo reads a story about a little house.
The children hear every word.
Their mothers and fathers come to find them.
They stand and hear the story too.
Then they look at the books.
Many of them buy something.
Now every Saturday is story day.
The little shop is full of people.
People talk and children ask for more stories.
Leo's father stands at the door and smiles.
"The big store has many things," he says.
But it does not have Leo.

quiz.q: What does the little shop sell?
options: Books and paper
Food and water
Shoes and coats

quiz.q: Why is Leo's father not happy?
options: Not many people come to the shop
Leo does not help him
The shop is too big

quiz.q: What does Leo do on Saturday?
options: He reads stories to children
He works at the big store
He plays with a dog

sentences.text: Leo is sixteen, and he lives in a small town.
His father has a little shop on the main street.
It sells books, paper, and pens.
The shop is old, and it smells of paper and wood.
Leo helps his father there after school and on Saturdays.
He knows where every book lives on the shelves.
Last year, a big new store opened near the station.
It is bright, loud, and full of cheap things.
People started to go there for everything.
Fewer and fewer people came to the little shop.
Some days, nobody came at all.
Leo's father sat behind the table and watched the door.
He smiled less and less every week.
One evening, Leo heard him talk to his mother.
"Maybe we are going to close the shop," he said quietly.
The shop was his grandfather's shop many years ago.
Leo lay in bed that night and could not sleep.
He wanted to do something, but he did not know what.
The next Saturday, Leo watched the street from the shop window.
Parents walked to the big store with fast steps.
Their children waited outside the store with nothing to do.
They looked at their shoes and at the sky.
Suddenly, Leo had an idea, and he smiled.
The next Saturday morning, he opened the shop very early.
He put small chairs in a circle near the window.
He wrote on a big paper: story time at ten.
At ten, he opened a book and started to read out loud.
First one child came in, then three, then seven.
His father watched him and said nothing.
Leo read a story about a dog that was lost at sea.
The children sat very still and listened.
Soon their parents came to look for them.
They stood at the back and listened too.
After the story, the parents looked around the shop.
Many of them bought a book or some paper.
Leo's father showed them the new books with a big smile.
Now every Saturday is story day at the little shop.
Children bring their friends, and parents bring their money.
Some children come early to get the best chairs.
Leo's father stands at the door and welcomes everyone.
"The big store sells everything," he says with a smile.
"But people do not go there to feel at home."
The little shop feels alive again, and so does its family.

quiz.q: What happened after the big store opened?
options: Fewer people came to the little shop
The little shop sold more books
Leo stopped helping his father

quiz.q: What did Leo's father say one evening?
options: Maybe they are going to close the shop
Maybe they are going to buy the big store
Maybe Leo should leave school

quiz.q: What did the parents do after story time?
options: They looked around and bought things
They went home quickly
They asked Leo to stop reading

sentences.text: Leo has grown up between the shelves of his father's little shop.
It stands on the main street of a small town and sells books and paper.
The place smells of wood and old pages, and the floor sings under your feet.
Since he was little, Leo has helped there after school and on weekends.
He knows every corner of the shop better than his own room.
Customers are called by their names, and their favorite books are remembered.
Everything changed when a huge store opened near the station last year.
It was bright and loud, and it sold everything cheaply.
Within a few months, most of the town was shopping there.
The little shop grew quieter and quieter, like a radio losing its signal.
Some days the doorbell did not ring even once.
Even the oldest customers began to visit less and less often.
Leo's father tried to hide his worry, but he was bad at hiding.
He counted the money quietly at night and stopped whistling in the mornings.
One evening Leo heard his parents talking in the kitchen.
"If this winter is like the autumn, we will have to close," his father said.
Leo went to bed with those words turning in his head.
He loved the shop too much to watch it die quietly.
He decided that he would rather try something and fail than only watch.
The next Saturday, he stood at the window and studied the street.
Parents hurried toward the big store, checking lists on their phones.
Their children trailed behind and then waited outside, bored and cold.
Nobody had planned anything for them, and they had nowhere to go.
Leo looked at the children, then at the empty chairs in the shop.
The idea arrived so suddenly that he laughed out loud.
He knew exactly what those children needed, because he had been one of them.
He spent the week preparing without telling his father the whole plan.
He cleaned the front corner and arranged small chairs in a circle.
He chose the best adventure stories from the children's shelf.
On the door he hung a simple sign: story time, Saturday at ten.
He practiced reading out loud in his room until his voice sounded strong.
His father read the sign twice and raised one eyebrow.
"Nobody comes here for stories," he said, but he did not take it down.
That Saturday at ten, Leo sat down and began to read out loud.
His voice shook a little at first, because the chairs were still empty.
Then the doorbell rang, and a small girl slipped inside.
By the second chapter, seven children were sitting in the circle.
Their parents arrived one by one, looking for them.
They stayed at the back, listening with their arms full of shopping bags.
When the story ended, something unexpected happened.
The parents did not hurry out; they walked slowly between the shelves.
They touched the books, asked questions, and remembered the shop from their own childhood.
Almost every family bought something before leaving.
Leo's father stood behind the counter, saying thank you again and again.
That evening, his father counted the money and whistled for the first time in months.
Story time has been running every Saturday since that morning.
Children save seats for their friends, and parents plan their shopping around it.
The big store near the station is still bigger, brighter, and cheaper.
Leo has already chosen the stories for the next three months.
But if you want a story and a warm hello, you know where to go.
The little shop is small, and that is exactly its power.

quiz.q: How did the big store change the town?
options: Most people started shopping there
It closed after a few months
It began selling books only

quiz.q: What did Leo's father say about the winter?
options: If it is like the autumn, they will have to close
Winter is the best season for the shop
They will move the shop to the station

quiz.q: Why did Leo start story time?
options: He saw bored children waiting outside the big store
His father asked him to do it
The school gave him the idea

sentences.text: Leo had spent his whole childhood between the wooden shelves of his father's shop.
It was a narrow place on the main street, where books and paper were sold to a patient town.
The floor sang a different note under every step, and the air always smelled of old pages.
Customers were greeted by name, and their tastes were remembered better than their addresses.
Leo helped after school, and he secretly believed the shop was the true center of the town.
On slow afternoons, his father would read to him behind the counter, one page at a time.
Those readings, Leo would later realize, were the best education he had ever received.
The trouble began when an enormous store was opened beside the station last spring.
It was the kind of place where everything could be found and nothing could be remembered.
Prices were cut, lights were bright, and music played from morning until night.
Within three months, the habits of the whole town had quietly changed.
People who had visited the little shop for twenty years now drove past it.
There were days when the doorbell stayed silent from opening until closing.
The shop's few remaining customers were old friends who came out of loyalty rather than need.
His father was not a man who complained, which made everything harder to watch.
He stopped whistling while he arranged the shelves, and he counted the money twice each night.
One evening, through the kitchen wall, Leo heard the sentence he had been afraid of.
"If the winter looks like the autumn, the shop will have to be closed," his father said.
His mother said something soft that Leo could not hear.
Leo lay awake for hours, staring at the ceiling and turning the problem over.
If the shop had been beaten by better books, he could have accepted it.
Being beaten by cheap lights and loud music felt like losing to nothing at all.
By morning he had decided that trying and failing would hurt less than watching and waiting.
The following Saturday, Leo stood at the window and watched the street like a scientist.
Parents marched toward the big store with lists in their hands and phones against their ears.
Their children were dragged along and then left waiting outside the glass doors.
They kicked small stones, stared at the sky, and looked thoroughly bored.
Nobody had built anything in the new store for them, because children carry no money.
Leo looked from the bored children to the empty corner of the shop, and the idea arrived complete.
He remembered his own afternoons behind the counter, and how a single voice had filled the whole shop.
He prepared all week, telling his father only half of the plan.
The front corner was cleared, the small chairs were arranged in a circle, and the best stories were chosen.
On Friday evening he hung a hand-written sign on the door: story time, Saturday at ten.
His father read it twice, opened his mouth, and closed it again.
"Nobody comes to a shop for stories," he finally said, though he left the sign where it was.
"People used to come here for exactly that," Leo answered, "they just called it browsing."
That night Leo practiced in his room, reading to a wall of imaginary children until midnight.
At ten the next morning, Leo sat facing seven empty chairs and began to read aloud.
His voice shook, because reading to empty chairs feels remarkably close to failing in public.
Then the doorbell rang, and a small girl in a red coat slipped quietly inside.
She was followed by two brothers, then by a boy who pretended he was not interested.
By the second chapter, every chair was taken and two children sat on the floor.
Their parents appeared one by one, drawn away from the big store by their own children.
They stood at the back with heavy bags, and none of them left before the ending.
When Leo finally closed the book, there was a silence that no big store could ever sell.
What happened after the story surprised even Leo.
The parents wandered between the shelves as if they had entered a museum of their own childhood.
Books were picked up, opened, smelled, and carried to the counter in small piles.
A woman found the exact edition she had owned at nine and bought it without asking the price.
Leo's father said thank you so many times that the words stopped sounding like words.
That evening, the money was counted only once, and it was counted smiling.
A man bought a dictionary he clearly did not need, simply because his daughter refused to leave.
Story time has been held every Saturday since, and it has slowly changed the shop's place in town.
Children reserve seats for friends, teachers send their classes, and parents arrange their shopping around it.
Other shop owners on the street have started asking Leo, only half joking, to read in their windows too.
The big store is still bigger, brighter, and cheaper, and it always will be.
But a town, Leo has learned, does not gather around the cheapest lights.
It gathers wherever somebody knows its name and keeps a chair ready.
His father still says that nobody comes to a shop for stories.
He says it every Saturday, smiling, while he carries more chairs to the front of the shop.

quiz.q: Why was losing to the big store especially painful for Leo?
options: The shop was losing to lights and music, not to better books
His father blamed him for the empty shop
The big store had copied their story time

quiz.q: Why had the big store built nothing for children?
options: Children carry no money
The town had no children
The law did not allow it

quiz.q: What does Leo conclude about the town at the end?
options: It gathers where somebody knows its name
It always chooses the cheapest store
It no longer needs small shops
